# Numbers Chapter 21 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 173–174; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 128–129 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_021_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_021_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — SERPENTS, SONGS, AND SWORDS: THE TURNING OF ISRAEL'S FORTUNES:** Numbers 21 marks a decisive pivot in Israel's wilderness narrative. After nearly forty years of defeat, death, and murmuring, this chapter records a sequence of reversals: from defeat to victory, from complaint to praise, from serpent-bite to serpent-healing. The chapter opens with the Canaanite king of Arad attacking Israel (vv. 1–3), leading to a vow of total destruction (kherem) — Israel's first offensive military action in the narrative. The infamous bronze serpent episode follows (vv. 4–9), where YHWH's judgment through fiery serpents is met by YHWH's own provision of healing through a serpent set upon a pole. The journey itinerary then resumes (vv. 10–20), punctuated by the Song of the Well (vv. 17–18), one of the oldest poetic fragments in the Hebrew Bible. The chapter closes with two decisive military victories: over Sihon king of the Amorites (vv. 21–32) and Og king of Bashan (vv. 33–35), conquests that will provide the Transjordanian inheritance for Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. In the Leningrad Codex, Numbers 21 begins on page 173 (continuing from the Edom narrative of chapter 20) and extends onto page 174. The Vaticanus LXX preserves the chapter across pages 128–129 (pa_0128 to pa_0129). The parashah Chuqat (חֻקַּת) continues through v. 20, and parashah Balak (בָּלָק) begins later; some traditions place a division at v. 21 with the new section on Sihon. A parashah marker ס (closed section) appears in the Leningrad Codex between sections of the journey narrative.

---

## The Defeat of the Canaanite King of Arad (vv. 1–3)

**1** And-heard [va-yishma] the-Canaanite [ha-Kena'ani], the-king of [melekh] Arad [Arad], dwelling [yoshev] the-Negev [ha-Negev], that [ki] came [ba] Israel [Yisra'el] [by] the-way of [derekh] the-Atharim [ha-Atarim]; and-he-fought [va-yillakhem] against-Israel [be-Yisra'el], and-he-took-captive [va-yishb] from-them [mimmennu] a-captive [shevi].

> **Key terms:** הַכְּנַעֲנִי מֶלֶךְ עֲרָד (ha-Kena'ani melekh Arad) = "the Canaanite, king of Arad" — Arad is a city in the eastern Negev. Archaeological work at Tel Arad has uncovered both Early Bronze and Iron Age strata. יֹשֵׁב הַנֶּגֶב (yoshev ha-Negev) = "dwelling [in] the Negev" — the participial form specifies the southern desert region as his domain. דֶּרֶךְ הָאֲתָרִים (derekh ha-Atarim) = "the way of the Atharim" — an obscure toponym. The meaning is debated: some derive it from אתר (atar, "to track/spy"), suggesting "the way of the spies," recalling the reconnaissance route of Numbers 13–14. Others take it as a proper place name. וַיִּשְׁבְּ מִמֶּנּוּ שֶׁבִי (va-yishb mimmennu shevi) = "and he took captive from them a captive" — the cognate accusative (שׁבה + שְׁבִי) intensifies the action: "he took captiving a captivity." **LXX:** καὶ ἤκουσεν ὁ Χανανις βασιλεὺς Αραδ ... ὁ κατοικῶν κατὰ τὴν ἔρημον ("and the Canaanite king Arad heard ... the one dwelling by the desert") — the LXX reads κατὰ τὴν ἔρημον ("by the desert") rather than "the Negev." The Atharim is rendered ὁδὸν Αθαριν in some witnesses.

**2** And-vowed [va-yiddar] Israel [Yisra'el] a-vow [neder] to-YHWH, and-he-said [va-yomar]: "If [im] giving [naton] you-will-give [titten] [et] the-people [ha-am] the-this [ha-zeh] into-my-hand [be-yadi], and-I-will-devote-to-destruction [ve-hakharamti] [et] their-cities [areihem]."

> **Key terms:** וַיִּדַּר יִשְׂרָאֵל נֶדֶר (va-yiddar Yisra'el neder) = "and Israel vowed a vow" — cognate accusative reinforcing the solemnity of the vow. This is the first vow of kherem made by Israel as a collective body. אִם־נָתֹן תִּתֵּן (im-naton titten) = "if giving you will give" — the **infinitive absolute** (נָתֹן, naton) preceding the finite verb (תִּתֵּן, titten) creates emphasis: "if you will surely give." וְהַחֲרַמְתִּי (ve-hakharamti) = "and I will devote to destruction" — the hiphil of חרם (kh-r-m), meaning to place under the ban (kherem), devoting everything to YHWH by total destruction. This practice is associated with holy war (cf. Deut 7:2; 20:17; Josh 6:17–21). **LXX:** καὶ ηὔξατο Ισραηλ εὐχὴν κυρίῳ ... καὶ ἀναθεματιῶ αὐτὸν καὶ τὰς πόλεις αὐτοῦ ("and Israel vowed a vow to [the] Lord ... and I will devote him and his cities to destruction").

**3** And-listened [va-yishma] YHWH to-the-voice of [be-qol] Israel [Yisra'el], and-he-gave [va-yitten] [et] the-Canaanite [ha-Kena'ani], and-he-devoted-to-destruction [va-yakharem] [et]-them [ethem] and-[et] their-cities [areihem]; and-he-called [va-yiqra] the-name of [shem] the-place [ha-maqom] Hormah [Khormah].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּשְׁמַע יְהוָה בְּקוֹל יִשְׂרָאֵל (va-yishma YHWH be-qol Yisra'el) = "and YHWH listened to the voice of Israel" — the preposition בְּ (be, "in/to") with שׁמע (shama, "to hear") indicates attentive, responsive hearing. וַיַּחֲרֵם אֶתְהֶם (va-yakharem ethem) = "and he devoted them to destruction" — the hiphil of חרם (kh-r-m), executing the vow of v. 2. חׇרְמָה (Khormah) = "Hormah" — an etiological naming derived from חֵרֶם (kherem, "ban/devotion to destruction"). The place name memorializes the act of total destruction. This same location was the site of Israel's earlier defeat when they presumed to enter the land without YHWH's sanction (Num 14:45). Now, with YHWH's support, the outcome is reversed. **LXX:** καὶ εἰσήκουσεν κύριος τῆς φωνῆς Ισραηλ καὶ παρέδωκεν τὸν Χανανιν ... καὶ ἐπωνόμασεν τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ τόπου ἐκείνου Ἀνάθεμα ("and [the] Lord hearkened to the voice of Israel and delivered the Canaanite ... and he named that place Anathema") — the LXX translates the place name rather than transliterating it, rendering חׇרְמָה as Ἀνάθεμα ("devoted thing/ban").

---

## The Bronze Serpent (vv. 4–9)

**4** And-they-journeyed [va-yis'u] from-Mount [me-Hor] Hor [ha-Hor] [by] the-way of [derekh] the-Sea of [Yam] Reeds [Suf], to-go-around [lisov] [et] the-land of [erets] Edom [Edom]; and-was-short [va-tiqtsar] the-soul of [nefesh] the-people [ha-am] on-the-way [ba-darekh].

> **Key terms:** מֵהֹר הָהָר (me-Hor ha-Har) = "from Mount Hor" — literally "from Hor the mountain," the site of Aaron's death (Num 20:22–29). דֶּרֶךְ יַם־סוּף (derekh Yam-Suf) = "the way of the Sea of Reeds" — forced to detour southward toward the Gulf of Aqaba because Edom refused passage (Num 20:21). This southward march, away from the promised land, is the immediate cause of the people's impatience. וַתִּקְצַר נֶפֶשׁ־הָעָם בַּדָּרֶךְ (va-tiqtsar nefesh-ha-am ba-darekh) = "and the soul of the people grew short on the way" — the idiom קצר נפשׁ (qatsar nefesh, "shortness of soul") expresses impatience, frustration, and spiritual exhaustion (cf. Judg 10:16; 16:16). The "soul" (נֶפֶשׁ, nefesh) here denotes the seat of vitality and endurance. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξάραντες ἐξ Ωρ τοῦ ὄρους ὁδὸν ἐπὶ θάλασσαν ἐρυθράν ... καὶ ὠλιγοψύχησεν ὁ λαὸς ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ ("and setting out from Hor the mountain [by the] way toward the Red Sea ... and the people became faint-hearted on the way") — the LXX renders the idiom with ὠλιγοψύχησεν ("became small-souled/faint-hearted").

**5** And-spoke [va-yedabber] the-people [ha-am] against-God [be-Elohim] and-against [u-ve] Moses [Mosheh]: "Why [lammah] have-you-brought-us-up [he'elitanu] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim] to-die [lamut] in-the-wilderness [ba-midbar]? For-there-is-no [ki-ein] bread [lekhem] and-there-is-no [ve-ein] water [mayim], and-our-soul [ve-nafshenu] loathes [qatsah] the-bread [ba-lekhem] the-contemptible [ha-qeloqel]."

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר הָעָם בֵּאלֹהִים וּבְמֹשֶׁה (va-yedabber ha-am be-Elohim u-ve-Mosheh) = "and the people spoke against God and against Moses" — unusually, the complaint is directed against God directly, not merely against Moses as human intermediary. The preposition בְּ (be) with דבר (d-b-r) denotes hostile speech ("spoke against"). לָמָּה הֶעֱלִיתָנוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם (lammah he'elitanu mi-Mitsrayim) = "why have you brought us up from Egypt" — the recurring exodus-complaint (cf. Exod 14:11; 16:3; 17:3; Num 11:5; 20:5). הַלֶּחֶם הַקְּלֹקֵל (ha-lekhem ha-qeloqel) = "the contemptible bread" — from the root קלל (q-l-l, "to be light/contemptible"), the polpel form קְלֹקֵל (qeloqel) intensifies the disdain. This likely refers to the manna (cf. Num 11:6). The self-contradiction — "there is no bread" yet "our soul loathes the bread" — captures the irrational nature of the complaint. **LXX:** κατελάλει ὁ λαὸς πρὸς τὸν θεὸν καὶ κατὰ Μωυσῆ ... καὶ ἡ ψυχὴ ἡμῶν προσώχθισεν ἐν τῷ ἄρτῳ τῷ διακένῳ ("the people spoke against God and against Moses ... and our soul is disgusted with the worthless bread") — the LXX renders הַקְּלֹקֵל as τῷ διακένῳ ("the empty/worthless").

**6** And-sent [va-yeshallakh] YHWH against-the-people [ba-am] [et] the-serpents [ha-nekhashim] the-fiery [ha-serafim]; and-they-bit [va-yenashekhu] [et] the-people [ha-am], and-died [va-yamot] much [rav] people [am] from-Israel [mi-Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** הַנְּחָשִׁים הַשְּׂרָפִים (ha-nekhashim ha-serafim) = "the fiery serpents" — נָחָשׁ (nakhash, "serpent") modified by שָׂרָף (saraf, "fiery/burning one"), describing either the burning sensation of their venom or their appearance. The root שׂרף (s-r-p) means "to burn"; the noun שָׂרָף (saraf) is the same word used for the celestial beings in Isaiah 6:2, 6 — the seraphim. This lexical connection between venomous desert serpents and heavenly attendants is one of the more striking features of biblical Hebrew. In Deuteronomy 8:15, Moses recalls "the fiery serpent and the scorpion" as wilderness dangers. וַיְנַשְּׁכוּ (va-yenashekhu) = "and they bit" — from נשׁך (nashakh, "to bite"), specifically of serpent bites (Gen 49:17; Amos 5:19; 9:3). **LXX:** καὶ ἀπέστειλεν κύριος εἰς τὸν λαὸν τοὺς ὄφεις τοὺς θανατοῦντας ("and [the] Lord sent among the people the deadly serpents") — the LXX renders הַשְּׂרָפִים not as "fiery" but as τοὺς θανατοῦντας ("the death-dealing ones"), interpreting the adjective by its lethal effect rather than its etymological sense.

**7** And-came [va-yavo] the-people [ha-am] unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], and-they-said [va-yomeru]: "We-have-sinned [khatanu], for [ki] we-spoke [dibbarnu] against-YHWH [ba-YHWH] and-against-you [u-vakha]; pray [hitpallel] unto [el] YHWH, and-let-him-remove [ve-yaser] from-upon-us [me-aleinu] [et] the-serpent [ha-nakhash]." And-prayed [va-yitpallel] Moses [Mosheh] on-behalf-of [be'ad] the-people [ha-am].

> **Key terms:** חָטָאנוּ (khatanu) = "we have sinned" — from חטא (khata, "to miss the mark/sin"). This is a rare instance of genuine collective confession in the wilderness narratives. כִּי דִבַּרְנוּ בַיהוָה וּבָךְ (ki dibbarnu va-YHWH u-vakha) = "for we spoke against YHWH and against you" — they repeat the charge of v. 5, acknowledging their own guilt. הִתְפַּלֵּל (hitpallel) = "pray" — the hitpael imperative of פלל (p-l-l), meaning "intercede." Moses functions as intercessor, a role established in Exodus 32:11–14 and Numbers 14:13–19. וְיָסֵר מֵעָלֵינוּ אֶת־הַנָּחָשׁ (ve-yaser me-aleinu et-ha-nakhash) = "and let him remove from upon us the serpent" — the singular הַנָּחָשׁ (ha-nakhash) is collective, referring to the serpent-plague as a whole. **LXX:** ἡμάρτομεν ὅτι κατελαλήσαμεν κατὰ τοῦ κυρίου καὶ κατὰ σοῦ· εὖξαι ... πρὸς κύριον, καὶ ἀφελέτω ἀφ᾽ ἡμῶν τὸν ὄφιν ("we have sinned because we spoke against [the] Lord and against you; pray ... to [the] Lord, and let him take away from us the serpent").

**8** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "Make [aseh] for-yourself [lekha] a-fiery-serpent [saraf], and-set [ve-sim] [et]-it [oto] upon [al] a-pole [nes]; and-it-shall-be [ve-hayah], every-one [kol] the-bitten [ha-nashukh] and-he-shall-see [ve-ra'ah] [et]-it [oto], and-he-shall-live [va-khai]."

> **Key terms:** עֲשֵׂה לְךָ שָׂרָף (aseh lekha saraf) = "make for yourself a fiery-serpent" — YHWH does not command the removal of the serpents but provides a means of healing within the ongoing danger. The instruction is paradoxical: the image of the affliction becomes the instrument of deliverance. וְשִׂים אֹתוֹ עַל־נֵס (ve-sim oto al-nes) = "and set it upon a pole" — נֵס (nes) typically means "banner, standard, signal-pole" (cf. Isa 5:26; 11:10; 13:2; 49:22; 62:10). The serpent is to be publicly displayed, elevated where all can see it. כׇּל־הַנָּשׁוּךְ (kol-ha-nashukh) = "every one bitten" — the qal passive participle of נשׁך (nashakh); the remedy is available to all without exception. וְרָאָה אֹתוֹ וָחָי (ve-ra'ah oto va-khai) = "and he shall see it and he shall live" — the healing is mediated through sight: looking at the bronze serpent with faith brings life. This passage is cited in John 3:14–15, where Jesus draws an analogy: "As Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up." **LXX:** ποίησον σεαυτῷ ὄφιν καὶ θὲς αὐτὸν ἐπὶ σημείου ("make for yourself a serpent and place it upon a sign/standard") — the LXX renders שָׂרָף simply as ὄφιν ("serpent") and נֵס as σημεῖον ("sign"), which carries theological weight: the serpent on the pole is a "sign."

**9** And-made [va-ya'as] Moses [Mosheh] a-serpent of [nakhash] bronze [nekhoshet], and-he-set [va-yesimehu] it [=] upon [al] the-pole [ha-nes]; and-it-was [ve-hayah], if [im] bit [nashakh] the-serpent [ha-nakhash] [et] a-man [ish], and-he-looked [ve-hibbit] unto [el] the-serpent of [nakhash] the-bronze [ha-nekhoshet], and-he-lived [va-khai].

> **Key terms:** נְחַשׁ נְחֹשֶׁת (nakhash nekhoshet) = "a serpent of bronze" — the deliberate wordplay between נָחָשׁ (nakhash, "serpent") and נְחֹשֶׁת (nekhoshet, "bronze/copper") is unmistakable. The phonetic similarity binds the creature to its metal image. Moses chooses bronze as the material, though YHWH specified only שָׂרָף (saraf, "fiery serpent"); the bronze color may evoke the reddish-copper hue of the desert vipers. וְהִבִּיט אֶל־נְחַשׁ הַנְּחֹשֶׁת (ve-hibbit el-nakhash ha-nekhoshet) = "and he looked at the serpent of bronze" — the hiphil of נבט (n-b-t, "to look"), denoting an intentional, focused gaze. The Mishnah interprets: "Does a serpent kill or a serpent give life? Rather, when Israel looked upward and subjected their hearts to their Father in heaven, they were healed" (m. Rosh Hashanah 3:8). This bronze serpent survived until the reign of Hezekiah, who destroyed it because the people were burning incense to it, calling it Nehushtan (2 Kgs 18:4). **LXX:** καὶ ἐποίησεν Μωυσῆς ὄφιν χαλκοῦν καὶ ἔστησεν αὐτὸν ἐπὶ σημείου ("and Moses made a bronze serpent and set it upon a sign") — the Hebrew wordplay (nakhash / nekhoshet) is lost in the Greek (ὄφιν χαλκοῦν).

---

## The Journey to Moab (vv. 10–20)

**10** And-journeyed [va-yis'u] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], and-they-encamped [va-yakhanu] in-Oboth [be-Ovot].

> **Key terms:** אֹבֹת (Ovot) = "Oboth" — a station on the wilderness itinerary, possibly related to אוֹב (ov, "spirit/medium"), though the connection is uncertain. The station list here parallels Numbers 33:43–44.

**11** And-they-journeyed [va-yis'u] from-Oboth [me-Ovot], and-they-encamped [va-yakhanu] in-Iye-abarim [be-Iyei ha-Avarim], in-the-wilderness [ba-midbar] which [asher] [is] upon [al] the-face of [penei] Moab [Mo'av], toward-the-rising of [mi-mizrakh] the-sun [ha-shamesh].

> **Key terms:** עִיֵּי הָעֲבָרִים (Iyei ha-Avarim) = "Iye-abarim" — literally "the ruins of the crossings/passages." The name עֲבָרִים (Avarim) is related to עבר (avar, "to cross over") and designates the mountain range east of the Jordan from which Moses will later view the land (Num 27:12; Deut 32:49). מִמִּזְרַח הַשָּׁמֶשׁ (mi-mizrakh ha-shamesh) = "from the rising of the sun" — i.e., to the east, specifying Moab's position relative to the wilderness. **LXX:** ἐν Αχελγαι, ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ ἥ ἐστιν κατὰ πρόσωπον Μωαβ κατὰ ἀνατολὰς ἡλίου ("in Achelgai, in the wilderness which is before Moab toward the rising of the sun").

**12** From-there [mi-sham] they-journeyed [nasa'u], and-they-encamped [va-yakhanu] in-the-Valley of [Nakhal] Zered [Zared].

> **Key terms:** נַחַל זָרֶד (Nakhal Zared) = "the Valley/Wadi of Zered" — the wadi marking the boundary between Edom and Moab (cf. Deut 2:13–14). The crossing of the Zered marks the end of the thirty-eight-year wandering period (Deut 2:14). **LXX:** ἐν φάραγγι Ζαρετ ("in the ravine of Zaret").

**13** From-there [mi-sham] they-journeyed [nasa'u], and-they-encamped [va-yakhanu] on-the-other-side of [me-ever] Arnon [Arnon], which [asher] [is] in-the-wilderness [ba-midbar] the-going-out [ha-yotse] from-the-border of [mi-gevul] the-Amorite [ha-Emori]; for [ki] Arnon [Arnon] [is] the-border of [gevul] Moab [Mo'av], between [bein] Moab [Mo'av] and-between [u-vein] the-Amorite [ha-Emori].

> **Key terms:** מֵעֵבֶר אַרְנוֹן (me-ever Arnon) = "on the other side of the Arnon" — the Arnon (modern Wadi el-Mujib) is a deep canyon flowing into the eastern shore of the Dead Sea. אֲשֶׁר בַּמִּדְבָּר הַיֹּצֵא מִגְּבוּל הָאֱמֹרִי (asher ba-midbar ha-yotse mi-gevul ha-Emori) = "which is in the wilderness going out from the border of the Amorite" — the relative clause specifies the particular stretch of the Arnon where it emerges from Amorite-controlled territory into the wilderness. כִּי אַרְנוֹן גְּבוּל מוֹאָב בֵּין מוֹאָב וּבֵין הָאֱמֹרִי (ki Arnon gevul Mo'av bein Mo'av u-vein ha-Emori) = "for the Arnon is the border of Moab, between Moab and between the Amorite" — this crucial geographical note explains why Israel could pass through this territory without violating Moabite sovereignty: the land north of the Arnon had been taken from Moab by the Amorites (v. 26), and Israel took it from the Amorites, not from Moab. **LXX:** ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ τῇ ἐξεχούσῃ ἀπὸ τῶν ὁρίων τῶν Αμορραίων· ἔστιν γὰρ Αρνων ὅρια Μωαβ ἀνὰ μέσον Μωαβ καὶ ἀνὰ μέσον τοῦ Αμορραίου ("in the wilderness that extends from the borders of the Amorites; for the Arnon is the border of Moab, between Moab and between the Amorite").

**14** Therefore [al-ken] it-is-said [ye'amar] in-the-Book of [be-Sefer] the-Wars of [Milkhamot] YHWH: "[et]-Vaheb [Vahev] in-Suphah [be-Sufah], and-[et] the-valleys of [nakhalei] Arnon [Arnon],

> **Key terms:** סֵפֶר מִלְחֲמֹת יְהוָה (Sefer Milkhamot YHWH) = "the Book of the Wars of YHWH" — a lost literary work, cited only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its name indicates it contained accounts of YHWH's martial acts on Israel's behalf, likely in poetic form. This citation is one of several references to now-lost Israelite writings (cf. the Book of Jashar, Josh 10:13; 2 Sam 1:18). אֶת־וָהֵב בְּסוּפָה (et-Vahev be-Sufah) = "Vaheb in Suphah" — the text is notoriously obscure. The fragment appears to begin mid-sentence (note the accusative particle אֶת), suggesting the biblical author is quoting a portion of a longer poem. וָהֵב (Vahev) is likely a place name, and סוּפָה (Sufah) may be related to סוּף (suf, "reed") or mean "storm/whirlwind." **LXX:** διὰ τοῦτο λέγεται ἐν βιβλίῳ Πόλεμος τοῦ κυρίου· τὴν Ζωοβ ἐφλόγισεν ("therefore it is said in the book, 'War of the Lord': Zoob he set on fire") — the LXX offers a very different reading of the obscure Hebrew, apparently understanding the text differently.

**15** And-the-slope of [ve-eshed] the-valleys [ha-nekhalim] which [asher] it-turns [natah] to-the-dwelling of [le-shevet] Ar [Ar], and-it-leans [ve-nish'an] to-the-border of [li-gevul] Moab [Mo'av]."

> **Key terms:** וְאֶשֶׁד הַנְּחָלִים (ve-eshed ha-nekhalim) = "and the slope/outpouring of the valleys" — אֶשֶׁד (eshed) occurs only here and in Deuteronomy 3:17; it may mean "slope, watershed, or outpouring." The poetic fragment describes the topography of the Arnon gorge system. אֲשֶׁר נָטָה לְשֶׁבֶת עָר (asher natah le-shevet Ar) = "which turns toward the dwelling of Ar" — עָר (Ar) is Moab's principal city or region (cf. Num 21:28; Deut 2:9, 18, 29; Isa 15:1). **LXX:** καὶ τοὺς χειμάρρους Αρνων ... ὃ κατέστησεν κατοικεῖν Ηρ· καὶ πρόσκειται τοῖς ὁρίοις Μωαβ ("and the torrents of Arnon ... which he established to dwell in Er; and it adjoins the borders of Moab").

**16** And-from-there [u-mi-sham] Beer [Be'erah] — she [hi] [is] the-well [ha-be'er] which [asher] said [amar] YHWH to-Moses [le-Mosheh]: "Gather [esof] [et] the-people [ha-am], and-I-will-give [ve-ettenah] to-them [lahem] water [mayim]."

> **Key terms:** בְּאֵרָה (Be'erah) = "Beer" — meaning "well." The name itself is the story: it is the well that YHWH promised. אֱסֹף אֶת־הָעָם וְאֶתְּנָה לָהֶם מָיִם (esof et-ha-am ve-ettenah lahem mayim) = "gather the people and I will give them water" — in contrast to the Meribah episode (Num 20:2–13), there is no complaint, no striking of rocks, and no transgression. YHWH simply promises water, and it comes. The simplicity of this episode stands in deliberate contrast to the preceding crises. **LXX:** καὶ ἐκεῖθεν τὸ φρέαρ· τοῦτό ἐστιν τὸ φρέαρ ὃ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· συνάγαγε τὸν λαόν, καὶ δώσω αὐτοῖς ὕδωρ ("and from there the well; this is the well which [the] Lord said to Moses: gather the people, and I will give them water").

**17** Then [az] sang [yashir] Israel [Yisra'el] [et] the-song [ha-shirah] the-this [ha-zot]: "Spring-up [ali], O-well [be'er]! Sing [ennu] to-it [lah]!

> **Key terms:** אָז יָשִׁיר יִשְׂרָאֵל (az yashir Yisra'el) = "then Israel sang" — the same formula that introduces the Song of the Sea (Exod 15:1: אָז יָשִׁיר מֹשֶׁה, az yashir Mosheh). The yiqtol (imperfect) form יָשִׁיר may be a "preterite yiqtol" or may carry volitional force. עֲלִי בְאֵר (ali be'er) = "spring up, O well!" — the imperative feminine singular of עלה (alah, "to go up/ascend"), addressed to the well as a personified feminine entity. עֱנוּ־לָהּ (ennu-lah) = "sing to it!" — the imperative plural of ענה (anah, "to respond/sing"), calling the community to antiphonal response to the well. This Song of the Well is one of the oldest poetic fragments in the Hebrew Bible, likely a pre-monarchic work song. **LXX:** τότε ᾖσεν Ισραηλ τὸ ᾆσμα τοῦτο ἐπὶ τοῦ φρέατος· ἐξάρχετε αὐτῷ ("then Israel sang this song at the well: begin singing to it!").

**18** Well [be'er] — [which] dug-it [khafaruha] princes [sarim]; excavated-it [kharuha] nobles of [nedivei] the-people [ha-am], with-the-scepter [bi-mekhoqeq], with-their-staffs [be-mish'anotam]." And-from-the-wilderness [u-mi-midbar], Mattanah [Mattanah];

> **Key terms:** חֲפָרוּהָ שָׂרִים כָּרוּהָ נְדִיבֵי הָעָם (khafaruha sarim, karuha nedivei ha-am) = "princes dug it, nobles of the people excavated it" — synonymous parallelism, the hallmark of Hebrew poetry. שָׂרִים (sarim, "princes") parallels נְדִיבֵי הָעָם (nedivei ha-am, "nobles of the people"). חָפַר (khafar, "to dig") parallels כָּרָה (karah, "to excavate/dig"). The well is not a natural spring but was ceremonially opened by Israel's leaders. בִּמְחֹקֵק בְּמִשְׁעֲנֹתָם (bi-mekhoqeq be-mish'anotam) = "with the scepter, with their staffs" — מְחֹקֵק (mekhoqeq) can mean "commander's staff/scepter" or "lawgiver" (cf. Gen 49:10; Deut 33:21; Judg 5:14). מִשְׁעֶנֶת (mish'enet) = "staff" — a walking staff, not a rod of authority. The leaders used their staffs of office to ceremonially break the ground. מַתָּנָה (Mattanah) = "Mattanah" — meaning "gift," beginning an itinerary that may also function as symbolic wordplay (gift > heights > valley). **LXX:** φρέαρ· ἔσκαψαν αὐτὸ ἄρχοντες, ἐξελατόμησαν αὐτὸ βασιλεῖς ἐθνῶν ἐν τῇ βασιλείᾳ αὐτῶν, ἐν τῷ κυριεῦσαι αὐτῶν ("a well; rulers dug it, kings of nations quarried it in their kingdom, in their dominion") — the LXX diverges significantly, reading "kings of nations" rather than "nobles of the people."

**19** And-from-Mattanah [u-mi-Mattanah], Nahaliel [Nakhali'el]; and-from-Nahaliel [u-mi-Nakhali'el], Bamoth [Bamot];

> **Key terms:** נַחֲלִיאֵל (Nakhali'el) = "Nahaliel" — meaning "valley/torrent of God" or "God's inheritance" (from נַחַל, nakhal, "wadi/torrent" + אֵל, El, "God"). בָּמוֹת (Bamot) = "Bamoth" — meaning "high places/heights." The sequence of place names — Mattanah ("gift"), Nahaliel ("torrent of God"), Bamoth ("heights") — may be read as a theological progression: from God's gift to God's torrent to the heights. Rabbinic exegesis (b. Eruvin 54a) interpreted the entire sequence allegorically: "The Torah was given as a gift (mattanah), became an inheritance of God (nahaliel), and from the inheritance of God [one rises to] heights (bamot)."

**20** And-from-Bamoth [u-mi-Bamot] [to] the-valley [ha-gai] which [asher] [is] in-the-field of [bi-sdeh] Moab [Mo'av], [at] the-head of [rosh] the-Pisgah [ha-Pisgah], and-it-looks-out [ve-nishqafah] upon [al] the-face of [penei] the-Jeshimon [ha-Yeshimon].

> **Key terms:** הַגַּיְ (ha-gai) = "the valley" — a narrow ravine or gorge. שְׂדֵה מוֹאָב (sdeh Mo'av) = "the field of Moab" — the open plateau region of Moab. רֹאשׁ הַפִּסְגָּה (rosh ha-Pisgah) = "the head/summit of Pisgah" — the elevated point from which Moses will later view the promised land (Deut 3:27; 34:1). Pisgah is part of the Abarim mountain range on the eastern shore of the Dead Sea. הַיְשִׁימֹן (ha-Yeshimon) = "the Jeshimon" — from יָשַׁם (yasham, "to be desolate"), meaning "the wasteland/desolation," referring to the barren wilderness stretching toward the Dead Sea. This final station positions Israel on the heights overlooking the Jordan valley and the promised land beyond. **LXX:** καὶ ἀπὸ Βαμωθ εἰς νάπην, ἥ ἐστιν ἐν τῷ πεδίῳ Μωαβ ἀπὸ κορυφῆς τοῦ λελαξευμένου τὸ βλέπον κατὰ πρόσωπον τῆς ἐρήμου ("and from Bamoth into a glen, which is in the plain of Moab, from the top of the hewn [cliff] looking toward the face of the wilderness").

## The Defeat of Sihon King of the Amorites (vv. 21–32)

**21** And-sent [va-yishlakh] Israel [Yisra'el] messengers [mal'akhim] unto [el] Sihon [Sikhon] king of [melekh] the-Amorite [ha-Emori], saying [le'mor]:

**22** "Let-me-pass [e'ebrah] through-your-land [be-artsekha]; not [lo] we-will-turn-aside [nitteh] into-a-field [be-sadeh] and-into-a-vineyard [u-ve-kherem]; not [lo] we-will-drink [nishteh] water of [mei] a-well [be'er]; on-the-road of [be-derekh] the-king [ha-melekh] we-will-walk [nelekh] until [ad] which [asher] we-pass [na'avor] [through] your-border [gevulekha]."

> **Key terms:** אֶעְבְּרָה בְאַרְצֶךָ (e'ebrah ve-artsekha) = "let me pass through your land" — the same diplomatic formula used with Edom (Num 20:17). The request is for transit, not territory. The first-person singular ("let me pass") represents the nation speaking as a corporate body. לֹא נִטֶּה בְּשָׂדֶה וּבְכֶרֶם (lo nitteh be-sadeh u-ve-kherem) = "we will not turn aside into a field or vineyard" — assurances of peaceful passage, pledging no agricultural damage. בְּדֶרֶךְ הַמֶּלֶךְ (be-derekh ha-melekh) = "on the King's Highway" — a major international trade route running north-south through Transjordan (cf. Num 20:17). The name may indicate a royal road maintained by the local king, or a main highway of the region. **LXX:** παρελευσόμεθα διὰ τῆς γῆς σου· οὐκ ἐκκλινοῦμεν οὔτε εἰς ἀγρὸν οὔτε εἰς ἀμπελῶνα, οὐ πιόμεθα ὕδωρ ἐκ φρέατός σου· ὁδῷ βασιλικῇ πορευσόμεθα ("we will pass through your land; we will turn aside neither into a field nor into a vineyard; we will not drink water from your well; by the royal road we will go").

**23** And-not [ve-lo] gave [natan] Sihon [Sikhon] [et] Israel [Yisra'el] to-pass [avor] through-his-border [bi-gevulo], and-gathered [va-ye'esof] Sihon [Sikhon] [et] all [kol] his-people [ammo], and-he-went-out [va-yetse] to-meet [liqrat] Israel [Yisra'el] [to] the-wilderness [ha-midbarah], and-he-came [va-yavo] to-Jahaz [Yahtsah], and-he-fought [va-yillakhem] against-Israel [be-Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** וְלֹא־נָתַן סִיחֹן אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל עֲבֹר בִּגְבֻלוֹ (ve-lo-natan Sikhon et-Yisra'el avor bi-gevulo) = "and Sihon did not give Israel [permission] to pass through his border" — the same refusal as Edom's (Num 20:21), but with different consequences: while Israel peacefully detoured around Edom (a brother-nation), Sihon's refusal leads to war. וַיֶּאֱסֹף סִיחֹן אֶת־כׇּל־עַמּוֹ (va-ye'esof Sikhon et-kol-ammo) = "and Sihon gathered all his people" — a full military mobilization. יָהְצָה (Yahtsah) = "Jahaz" — a city in the Moabite plateau; the Mesha Inscription (line 20) also mentions Jahaz (יהץ) as a place where Israel dwelt during its conflict with Moab. **LXX:** καὶ οὐκ ἔδωκεν Σηων τῷ Ισραηλ παρελθεῖν διὰ τῶν ὁρίων αὐτοῦ ... καὶ παρετάξατο τῷ Ισραηλ ἐν Ιασσα ("and Sihon did not allow Israel to pass through his borders ... and he arrayed himself against Israel at Iassa").

**24** And-struck-him [va-yakkehu] Israel [Yisra'el] by-the-mouth of [le-fi] the-sword [kherev], and-possessed [va-yirash] [et] his-land [artso] from-Arnon [me-Arnon] unto [ad] Jabbok [Yabboq], unto [ad] the-Sons of [benei] Ammon [Ammon]; for [ki] strong [az] [was] the-border of [gevul] the-Sons of [benei] Ammon [Ammon].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּכֵּהוּ יִשְׂרָאֵל לְפִי־חֶרֶב (va-yakkehu Yisra'el le-fi-kherev) = "and Israel struck him by the mouth of the sword" — the idiom לְפִי חֶרֶב (le-fi kherev, "by the mouth/edge of the sword") is among the most common battle-descriptions in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Gen 34:26; Josh 6:21; 8:24; 10:28). וַיִּירַשׁ אֶת־אַרְצוֹ (va-yirash et-artso) = "and possessed his land" — from ירשׁ (yarash, "to take possession/dispossess"). The conquered territory extends from the Arnon (southern border) to the Jabbok (modern Zarqa River, northern border). כִּי עַז גְּבוּל בְּנֵי עַמּוֹן (ki az gevul benei Ammon) = "for the border of the Sons of Ammon was strong" — the adjective עַז (az, "strong/fortified") explains why Israel's conquest halted at the Jabbok: the Ammonite frontier was too well-defended. Deuteronomy 2:19, 37 provides a theological explanation — YHWH forbade Israel from taking Ammonite territory. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπάταξεν αὐτὸν Ισραηλ φόνῳ μαχαίρης, καὶ κατεκυρίευσαν τῆς γῆς αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ Αρνων ἕως Ιαβοκ ἕως υἱῶν Αμμαν· ὅτι Ιαζηρ ὅρια υἱῶν Αμμαν ἐστίν ("and Israel struck him with the slaughter of the sword, and took possession of his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok, to the sons of Amman; for Jazer is the border of the sons of Amman") — the LXX reads "Jazer" (Ιαζηρ) instead of "strong" (עַז), apparently reading the consonants as a place name.

**25** And-took [va-yiqqakh] Israel [Yisra'el] [et] all [kol] the-cities [he-arim] the-these [ha-elleh]; and-dwelt [va-yeshev] Israel [Yisra'el] in-all [be-khol] the-cities of [arei] the-Amorite [ha-Emori], in-Heshbon [be-Kheshbon] and-in-all [u-ve-khol] her-daughters [benoteiha].

> **Key terms:** בְּחֶשְׁבּוֹן וּבְכׇל־בְּנֹתֶיהָ (be-Kheshbon u-ve-khol-benoteiha) = "in Heshbon and in all her daughters" — Heshbon is named as the principal city, with her "daughters" (בָּנוֹת, banot) being the dependent towns and villages in her administrative district. The metaphor of a mother-city and daughter-villages is standard in biblical geography (cf. Josh 15:45–47; 17:11; Judg 11:26; 2 Sam 20:19). Heshbon (modern Tell Hesban in Jordan) sits on the Moabite plateau approximately 20 miles east of the north end of the Dead Sea. **LXX:** καὶ ἔλαβεν Ισραηλ πάσας τὰς πόλεις ταύτας, καὶ κατῴκησεν Ισραηλ ἐν πάσαις ταῖς πόλεσιν τῶν Αμορραίων, ἐν Εσεβων καὶ ἐν πάσαις ταῖς συγκυρούσαις αὐτῇ ("and Israel took all these cities and dwelt in all the cities of the Amorites, in Esebon and in all [the cities] belonging to it").

**26** For [ki] Heshbon [Kheshbon] [was] the-city of [ir] Sihon [Sikhon] the-king of [melekh] the-Amorite [ha-Emori] — he [hu]; and-he [ve-hu] had-fought [nilkham] against-the-king of [be-melekh] Moab [Mo'av] the-former [ha-ri'shon], and-he-had-taken [va-yiqqakh] [et] all [kol] his-land [artso] from-his-hand [mi-yado] unto [ad] Arnon [Arnon].

> **Key terms:** כִּי חֶשְׁבּוֹן עִיר סִיחֹן מֶלֶךְ הָאֱמֹרִי הִוא (ki Kheshbon ir Sikhon melekh ha-Emori hi) = "for Heshbon was the city of Sihon king of the Amorite" — this verse provides the critical legal justification for Israel's territorial acquisition. The land between the Arnon and Jabbok had originally belonged to Moab, but Sihon had conquered it from מֶלֶךְ מוֹאָב הָרִאשׁוֹן (melekh Mo'av ha-ri'shon, "the former king of Moab"). Therefore Israel took the land from the Amorites, not from Moab, and YHWH's prohibition against seizing Moabite territory (Deut 2:9) was not violated. This argument is explicitly deployed in Judges 11:15–27, where Jephthah uses it in his diplomatic exchange with the king of Ammon. **LXX:** ὅτι Εσεβων πόλις Σηων τοῦ βασιλέως τῶν Αμορραίων ἐστίν· καὶ οὗτος ἐπολέμησεν βασιλέα Μωαβ τὸ πρότερον καὶ ἔλαβεν πᾶσαν τὴν γῆν αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ Αρνων ἕως Αροηρ ("for Esebon is [the] city of Sihon the king of the Amorites; and he had fought the king of Moab formerly and taken all his land from the Arnon to Aroer").

**27** Therefore [al-ken] say [yomeru] the-ones-speaking-proverbs [ha-moshelim]: "Come [bo'u] to-Heshbon [Kheshbon]; let-it-be-built [tibbanneh] and-let-it-be-established [ve-tikkonnen] the-city of [ir] Sihon [Sikhon].

> **Key terms:** הַמֹּשְׁלִים (ha-moshelim) = "the ones speaking proverbs/poets" — from מָשָׁל (mashal, "proverb, parable, byword, taunt-song"). These are professional poets or balladists who composed commemorative poems about notable events. The poem quoted in vv. 27–30 is an Amorite victory song, originally celebrating Sihon's conquest of Moab, which the biblical narrator now repurposes: the very city Sihon built on Moab's ruin has itself been conquered by Israel. The irony is devastating. בֹּאוּ חֶשְׁבּוֹן (bo'u Kheshbon) = "come to Heshbon" — an invitation to rebuild and inhabit the conquered city, originally addressed to Sihon's Amorite settlers. תִּבָּנֶה וְתִכּוֹנֵן (tibbanneh ve-tikkonnen) = "let it be built and let it be established" — jussive forms expressing the wish for the city's restoration and fortification. **LXX:** διὰ τοῦτο ἐροῦσιν οἱ αἰνιγματισταί· ἔλθατε εἰς Εσεβων, ἵνα οἰκοδομηθῇ καὶ κατασκευασθῇ πόλις Σηων ("therefore the riddlers shall say: come to Esebon, that the city of Sihon may be built and established").

**28** For [ki] a-fire [esh] went-out [yatse'ah] from-Heshbon [me-Kheshbon], a-flame [lehavah] from-the-city of [mi-qiryat] Sihon [Sikhon]; it-consumed [akhelah] Ar [Ar] of-Moab [Mo'av], the-lords of [ba'alei] the-high-places of [bamot] Arnon [Arnon].

> **Key terms:** כִּי־אֵשׁ יָצְאָה מֵחֶשְׁבּוֹן (ki-esh yatse'ah me-Kheshbon) = "for a fire went out from Heshbon" — fire as a metaphor for military devastation (cf. Judg 9:15, 20; Amos 1:4, 7, 10, 12, 14; 2:2, 5). לֶהָבָה מִקִּרְיַת סִיחֹן (lehavah mi-qiryat Sikhon) = "a flame from the city of Sihon" — synonymous parallelism: fire / flame, Heshbon / city of Sihon. אָכְלָה עָר מוֹאָב (akhelah Ar Mo'av) = "it consumed Ar of Moab" — Ar, the principal Moabite settlement (cf. v. 15; Isa 15:1), was destroyed by Sihon's campaign. בַּעֲלֵי בָּמוֹת אַרְנֹן (ba'alei bamot Arnon) = "the lords of the high places of the Arnon" — בַּעַל (ba'al) here means "lord, master, possessor" (not the deity); the phrase identifies the Moabite rulers who controlled the cult-heights along the Arnon gorge. **LXX:** ὅτι πῦρ ἐξῆλθεν ἐξ Εσεβων, φλὸξ ἐκ πόλεως Σηων· καὶ κατέφαγεν ἕως Μωαβ καὶ κατέπιεν στήλας Αρνων ("for a fire went out from Esebon, a flame from [the] city of Sihon; and it devoured as far as Moab and swallowed the pillars of Arnon").

**29** Woe [oi] to-you [lekha], Moab [Mo'av]! You-are-destroyed [avadta], O-people of [am] Chemosh [Kemosh]! He-has-given [natan] his-sons [banav] [as] fugitives [peletim], and-his-daughters [u-venotav] into-captivity [ba-shevit] to-the-king of [le-melekh] the-Amorite [Emori], Sihon [Sikhon].

> **Key terms:** אוֹי־לְךָ מוֹאָב (oi-lekha Mo'av) = "woe to you, Moab!" — the exclamation אוֹי (oi, "woe!") introduces a taunt or lament (cf. Isa 3:11; Jer 48:46, which quotes this very verse). עַם כְּמוֹשׁ (am Kemosh) = "people of Chemosh" — Chemosh was the national deity of Moab, attested both in the Hebrew Bible and in the Mesha Inscription (line 1: "I am Mesha ... king of Moab ... the Dibonite. My father reigned over Moab thirty years, and I reigned after my father. And I made this high place for Chemosh"). To call Moab the "people of Chemosh" is to identify them by their patron deity, who has failed to protect them. נָתַן בָּנָיו פְּלֵיטִם (natan banav peletim) = "he [Chemosh] has given his sons [as] fugitives" — the god Chemosh is the subject; he has abandoned his people, surrendering his devotees to flight and captivity. This theological mockery — a god who cannot save his own people — is characteristic of Israelite polemic against foreign deities. **LXX:** οὐαί σοι, Μωαβ· ἀπώλου, λαὸς Χαμως· ἀπέδοτο τοὺς υἱοὺς αὐτῶν διασώζεσθαι καὶ τὰς θυγατέρας αὐτῶν αἰχμαλώτους τῷ βασιλεῖ τῶν Αμορραίων Σηων ("woe to you, Moab; you are destroyed, people of Chamos; he sold their sons to be saved and their daughters as captives to the king of the Amorites, Sihon").

**30** And-we-shot [va-niram] [at] them; perished [avad] Heshbon [Kheshbon] unto [ad] Dibon [Divon], and-we-laid-waste [va-nashim] unto [ad] Nophah [Nofakh] which [asher] [reaches] unto [ad] Medeba [Medeva].

> **Key terms:** וַנִּירָם (va-niram) = "and we shot [at] them" — from ירה (yarah, "to shoot"), though the form is difficult and debated. Some emend to וַנַּשִּׁים (va-nashim) or read as from נִיר (nir, "to plow/devastate"). The text is among the most corrupt in Numbers; the Leningrad Codex reading stands as received. אָבַד חֶשְׁבּוֹן עַד דִּיבֹן (avad Kheshbon ad Divon) = "Heshbon perished unto Dibon" — Dibon (modern Dhiban) is approximately 13 miles south of Heshbon on the King's Highway. The Mesha Inscription was found at Dibon. וַנַּשִּׁים עַד נֹפַח אֲשֶׁר עַד מֵידְבָא (va-nashim ad Nofakh asher ad Medeva) = "and we laid waste unto Nophah which [reaches] unto Medeba" — Nophah is an unknown site; Medeba (modern Madaba) is a city on the Moabite plateau known for its later Byzantine mosaic map. The sweep of destruction covers the entire Moabite heartland from north to south. **LXX:** καὶ τὸ σπέρμα αὐτῶν ἀπολεῖται, Εσεβων ἕως Δαιβων· καὶ αἱ γυναῖκες ἔτι προσεξέκαυσαν πῦρ ἐπὶ Μωαβ ("and their seed shall perish, Esebon to Daibon; and the women yet kindled fire upon Moab") — the LXX diverges significantly here, apparently reading a different Vorlage or struggling with the same textual corruption visible in the MT.

**31** And-dwelt [va-yeshev] Israel [Yisra'el] in-the-land of [be-erets] the-Amorite [ha-Emori].

> **Key terms:** וַיֵּשֶׁב יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּאֶרֶץ הָאֱמֹרִי (va-yeshev Yisra'el be-erets ha-Emori) = "and Israel dwelt in the land of the Amorite" — a summary statement marking the completion of the conquest. Israel now occupies the Transjordanian territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok.

**32** And-sent [va-yishlakh] Moses [Mosheh] to-spy-out [le-raggel] [et] Jazer [Ya'zer], and-they-captured [va-yilkedu] [et] her-daughters [benoteiha], and-he-dispossessed [va-yoresh] [et] the-Amorite [ha-Emori] who [asher] [was] there [sham].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּשְׁלַח מֹשֶׁה לְרַגֵּל אֶת יַעְזֵר (va-yishlakh Mosheh le-raggel et Ya'zer) = "and Moses sent to spy out Jazer" — the piel infinitive construct of רגל (r-g-l, "to spy/reconnoiter"), the same root used for the disastrous reconnaissance mission of Numbers 13:2. Here the outcome is successful. Jazer (modern Khirbet es-Sar or Khirbet Jazzir) was an Amorite city north of Heshbon. וַיִּלְכְּדוּ אֶת בְּנֹתֶיהָ (va-yilkedu et benoteiha) = "and they captured her daughters" — the dependent villages of Jazer. וַיּוֹרֶשׁ אֶת הָאֱמֹרִי (va-yoresh et ha-Emori) = "and he dispossessed the Amorite" — the hiphil of ירשׁ (yarash, "to dispossess/drive out"). **LXX:** καὶ ἀπέστειλεν Μωυσῆς κατασκέψασθαι τὴν Ιαζηρ, καὶ κατελάβοντο αὐτὴν καὶ τὰς κώμας αὐτῆς, καὶ ἐξέβαλεν τὸν Αμορραῖον τὸν κατοικοῦντα ἐκεῖ ("and Moses sent to scout out Jazer, and they took it and its villages, and drove out the Amorite dwelling there").

## The Defeat of Og King of Bashan (vv. 33–35)

**33** And-they-turned [va-yifnu], and-they-went-up [va-ya'alu] [by the] way of [derekh] the-Bashan [ha-Bashan]; and-went-out [va-yetse] Og [Og] king of [melekh] the-Bashan [ha-Bashan] to-meet-them [liqratam], he [hu] and-all [ve-khol] his-people [ammo], to-the-battle [la-milkhamah] [at] Edrei [Edre'i].

> **Key terms:** וַיִּפְנוּ וַיַּעֲלוּ (va-yifnu va-ya'alu) = "and they turned and went up" — the verb פנה (panah, "to turn") indicates a change of direction; Israel now advances northward from the Heshbon plateau into the Bashan region. דֶּרֶךְ הַבָּשָׁן (derekh ha-Bashan) = "the way of Bashan" — the road leading northward to the fertile volcanic tableland of Bashan, east of the Sea of Galilee. עוֹג מֶלֶךְ הַבָּשָׁן (Og melekh ha-Bashan) = "Og king of Bashan" — Og is described in Deuteronomy 3:11 as the last of the Rephaim (giants), whose iron bedstead measured nine cubits by four cubits (approximately 13.5 x 6 feet). אֶדְרֶעִי (Edre'i) = "Edrei" — one of Og's royal cities (Deut 1:4; Josh 12:4), identified with modern Der'a in southern Syria. **LXX:** καὶ ἐπιστρέψαντες ἀνέβησαν ὁδὸν τὴν εἰς Βασαν· καὶ ἐξῆλθεν Ωγ βασιλεὺς τῆς Βασαν εἰς συνάντησιν αὐτοῖς ... εἰς Εδραιν ("and turning they went up [by] the way to Bashan; and Og king of Bashan went out to meet them ... at Edrain").

**34** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh]: "Do-not [al] fear [tira] [et]-him [oto], for [ki] into-your-hand [be-yadekha] I-have-given [natatti] [et]-him [oto] and-[et] all [kol] his-people [ammo] and-[et] his-land [artso]; and-you-shall-do [ve-asita] to-him [lo] as-which [ka'asher] you-did [asita] to-Sihon [le-Sikhon] king of [melekh] the-Amorite [ha-Emori] who [asher] was-dwelling [yoshev] in-Heshbon [be-Kheshbon]."

> **Key terms:** אַל־תִּירָא אֹתוֹ (al-tira oto) = "do not fear him" — the divine war oracle, a standard formula of encouragement before battle (cf. Deut 3:2; 20:3; 31:6, 8; Josh 8:1; 10:8; 11:6). The need for this reassurance suggests that Og's reputation as a giant (Rephaim) was a source of real fear for Israel. כִּי בְיָדְךָ נָתַתִּי אֹתוֹ (ki ve-yadekha natatti oto) = "for into your hand I have given him" — the perfect tense נָתַתִּי (natatti, "I have given") expresses divine certainty: the victory is already accomplished in YHWH's decree, even before the battle begins. This is the prophetic perfect, treating the future as already completed. וְעָשִׂיתָ לּוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ לְסִיחֹן (ve-asita lo ka'asher asita le-Sikhon) = "and you shall do to him as you did to Sihon" — the pattern established by the first Transjordanian conquest becomes the template for the second. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· μὴ φοβηθῇς αὐτόν, ὅτι εἰς τὰς χεῖράς σου παραδέδωκα αὐτὸν καὶ πάντα τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ καὶ πᾶσαν τὴν γῆν αὐτοῦ ("and [the] Lord said to Moses: do not fear him, for into your hands I have delivered him and all his people and all his land").

**35** And-they-struck [va-yakku] [et]-him [oto] and-[et] his-sons [banav] and-[et] all [kol] his-people [ammo] until [ad] not [bilti] he-had-remaining [hish'ir] to-him [lo] a-survivor [sarid]; and-they-possessed [va-yireshu] [et] his-land [artso].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּכּוּ אֹתוֹ וְאֶת בָּנָיו וְאֶת כׇּל עַמּוֹ (va-yakku oto ve-et banav ve-et kol ammo) = "and they struck him and his sons and all his people" — the verb נכה (n-k-h, "to strike/smite") in the hiphil describes military defeat. The destruction includes the royal house (בָּנָיו, banav, "his sons") and the entire population. עַד בִּלְתִּי הִשְׁאִיר לוֹ שָׂרִיד (ad bilti hish'ir lo sarid) = "until he had no survivor remaining to him" — the phrase בִּלְתִּי הִשְׁאִיר (bilti hish'ir, "not leaving remaining," hiphil of שׁאר) with שָׂרִיד (sarid, "survivor/remnant") expresses total annihilation (cf. Num 24:19; Deut 2:34; 3:3; Josh 8:22; 10:28, 30, 33, 37, 39, 40). וַיִּירְשׁוּ אֶת אַרְצוֹ (va-yireshu et artso) = "and they possessed his land" — with Og's defeat, Israel now controls the entire Transjordanian corridor from the Arnon in the south to the slopes of Mount Hermon in the north (Deut 3:8–10), encompassing the territories of Sihon (Heshbon-centered) and Og (Bashan). This vast territory will be allotted to the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh (Num 32). **LXX:** καὶ ἐπάταξεν αὐτὸν καὶ τοὺς υἱοὺς αὐτοῦ καὶ πάντα τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ, ἕως τοῦ μὴ καταλιπεῖν αὐτοῦ ζωγρίαν· καὶ ἐκληρονόμησαν τὴν γῆν αὐτοῦ ("and he struck him and his sons and all his people, until none of his were left alive; and they inherited his land").

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **The Book of the Wars of YHWH (v. 14):** The citation from this lost work is the only such reference in all of Scripture. The fragment quoted (vv. 14b–15) is among the most textually corrupt passages in the Hebrew Bible. The opening אֶת וָהֵב בְּסוּפָה (et Vahev be-Sufah) begins with an accusative marker, indicating the quotation starts mid-sentence. The original context, genre, and extent of this work are unrecoverable. Ancient interpreters struggled with the passage: the LXX offers a markedly different reading, and the Targumim paraphrase freely. The Leningrad Codex preserves the consonantal text faithfully, though the vocalization may represent medieval guesswork at an already obscure tradition.

2. **The Song of the Well (vv. 17–18):** This brief poem bears all the marks of genuine antiquity: (a) it is introduced with the archaic formula אָז יָשִׁיר (az yashir), shared only with the Song of the Sea (Exod 15:1); (b) it employs the imperative address to a natural element (the well), reminiscent of Judges 5:12; (c) its parallelism is terse and compact. The song may have originated as a work-song sung at well-digging, later incorporated into the wilderness tradition. The Vaticanus LXX renders the passage with slight differences but preserves the poetic structure.

3. **The Taunt Song against Moab (vv. 27–30):** The narrator introduces an Amorite victory ballad — originally celebrating Sihon's conquest of Moab — and repurposes it with devastating irony. The poet who sang "come to Heshbon, let it be built" for Sihon now unwittingly celebrates Sihon's own downfall: the city he built on Moab's ruin has been taken by Israel. Verse 29 is quoted nearly verbatim in Jeremiah 48:45–46, demonstrating the poem's long literary afterlife. The identification of Moab as "the people of Chemosh" (עַם כְּמוֹשׁ) is confirmed extrabiblically by the Mesha Inscription (9th century BCE), where Mesha credits Chemosh with both victory and defeat.

4. **Textual corruption in v. 30:** The MT of v. 30 is notoriously difficult. The form וַנִּירָם (va-niram) has no clear parsing; proposals include: (a) "and we shot at them" (from ירה, yarah); (b) "and their posterity perished" (emending to וְנִירָם, ve-niram, from נִיר, "lamp/posterity"); (c) "and we laid waste to them" (from נָוָה or related root). The LXX appears to read a different text entirely. The Leningrad Codex reading is preserved as received; the Vaticanus offers no resolution. This is a passage where honest acknowledgment of obscurity is more faithful than confident translation.

5. **The nakhash-nekhoshet wordplay (v. 9):** The paronomasia between נָחָשׁ (nakhash, "serpent") and נְחֹשֶׁת (nekhoshet, "bronze") is a deliberate literary device that functions only in Hebrew. The bronze serpent (נְחַשׁ הַנְּחֹשֶׁת, nakhash ha-nekhoshet) survived as a cult object until Hezekiah's reform (2 Kgs 18:4), where it is called נְחֻשְׁתָּן (Nekhushtan) — a name that fuses serpent and bronze into a single word. The LXX renders ὄφιν χαλκοῦν ("bronze serpent"), losing the wordplay entirely. The Vaticanus preserves this standard LXX reading.

6. **LXX divergence in v. 24 — "Jazer" for "strong":** The Vaticanus LXX reads Ιαζηρ (Jazer) where the MT has עַז (az, "strong"), apparently understanding the consonants עז as a place name rather than an adjective. This is a consequential variant: the MT says "the border of Ammon was strong [i.e., fortified]," explaining why Israel stopped at the Jabbok; the LXX says "Jazer is the border of Ammon," providing a different geographical marker. The Leningrad Codex reading (עַז, "strong") is followed in this translation.

7. **LXX rendering of שְׂרָפִים (v. 6):** The Vaticanus translates הַנְּחָשִׁים הַשְּׂרָפִים as τοὺς ὄφεις τοὺς θανατοῦντας ("the death-dealing serpents") rather than "the fiery serpents." This interpretive rendering focuses on the serpents' lethal effect rather than the etymological meaning of שָׂרָף ("burning one"). The MT's שְׂרָפִים preserves the semantic link to the celestial seraphim of Isaiah 6, a connection the LXX obscures.

8. **Og and the Rephaim (v. 33):** The text introduces Og without explanation, but Deuteronomy 3:11 supplies the tradition that he was the last of the Rephaim, possessing an iron bedstead of extraordinary size. The divine oracle of reassurance in v. 34 ("do not fear him") is unusual for a narrative where Israel has just defeated Sihon — unless Og's reputation as a giant made him uniquely terrifying. The Vaticanus LXX preserves the oracle faithfully.

9. **Parashah and section markers:** In the Leningrad Codex, section markers (ס and פ) appear at several points in Numbers 21, notably between the Arad episode (vv. 1–3) and the bronze serpent narrative (vv. 4–9), and between the itinerary (vv. 10–20) and the Sihon narrative (vv. 21–32). These divisions reflect ancient liturgical and literary traditions about the chapter's internal structure.
